history.en: Abelia grandiflora is a garden plant valued for seasonal interest and landscape structure. Botanical sources associate it with a wide range of regions.

habit.en: A garden plant grown outdoors. Provide sun appropriate to the species, water deeply during establishment, and improve drainage to prevent root issues.

native_habitat.en: Often associated with temperate woodland edges and shrublands with seasonal temperature swings.

notes.en: After establishment, water during dry spells; newly planted perennials need more consistent moisture.
Compost in spring is often enough; fertilize lightly based on growth and bloom.

soil.mix: Well-drained garden soil improved with compost.

how.en: Prune to shape and remove dead wood; avoid removing next season’s buds.

prevention: Water at soil level; space plants for airflow; remove diseased debris.

actions: Water early; mulch; provide temporary shade for new plantings.
Mulch crowns; protect early growth; cover tender shoots during late frosts.
Stake tall flowers; cut back damaged stems; remove debris after storms.
Improve drainage; avoid compacting wet soil; watch for fungal outbreaks.

hot_dry: Mulch 5–8 cm; use drip irrigation; select drought-tolerant companions.
cool_wet: Improve drainage with raised beds; avoid heavy clay compaction; prune for airflow.

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Acer palmatum is a garden plant valued for seasonal interest and landscape structure. Botanical sources associate it with a wide range of regions.

habit.en: A garden plant grown outdoors. Provide sun appropriate to the species, water deeply during establishment, and improve drainage to prevent root issues.

native_habitat.en: Typically from temperate forests and parklands with cold winters and warm summers.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.
Young trees need consistent deep watering during establishment; mature trees are watered during dry spells.
Fertilize in early spring based on soil test and vigor; avoid late-season nitrogen.

soil.mix: Well-drained loam; avoid waterlogged sites.

when.en: Late winter; structural pruning for young trees.

how.en: Prune for structure and clearance; remove dead/damaged limbs; avoid topping.

prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when possible.
Prune for airflow and sanitize tools.

actions: Deep water early; mulch root zone; protect young trees from sunscald.
Protect blossoms from late spring frosts with covers/sprinkling where appropriate.
Stake young trees; thin overloaded branches; inspect after wind for splits.
Improve drainage; avoid working saturated soil; monitor for root issues.

cool_short_season: Select low-chill / early varieties; plant in full sun; use windbreaks.
hot_humid: Prune for airflow; monitor fungal disease; use mulch to moderate soil moisture swings.

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Achillea millefolium is a garden plant valued for seasonal interest and landscape structure. Botanical sources associate it with a wide range of regions.

habit.en: A garden plant grown outdoors. Provide sun appropriate to the species, water deeply during establishment, and improve drainage to prevent root issues.

native_habitat.en: Often from meadows, woodland edges, and temperate garden-like habitats with seasonal cycles.

Compost in spring is often enough; fertilize lightly based on growth and bloom.

soil.mix: Well-drained garden soil improved with compost.

when.en: After bloom or late winter (varies by plant).

how.en: Remove spent flowers; cut back dead stems; divide clumps as needed.

prevention: Water at soil level; space plants for airflow; remove diseased debris.

history.en: Actinidia deliciosa is widely cultivated in gardens and farms. It is associated with central Southern China, growing naturally at altitudes between 800 and 1,400 metres (2,600 and 4,600 ft) in botanical sources and is grown today for its practical value and ornamental presence.

habit.en: A fruiting tree grown outdoors in sun. Water deeply during establishment, prune for structure and airflow, and protect blossoms from extreme weather.

native_range.en: central Southern China, growing naturally at altitudes between 800 and 1,400 metres (2,600 and 4,600 ft)

native_habitat.en: Associated with temperate orchards and woodland edges; requires full sun for best flowering and fruiting.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.
Young trees need consistent deep watering during establishment; mature trees are watered during dry spells.
Fertilize in early spring based on soil test and vigor; avoid late-season nitrogen.

soil.mix: Well-drained loam; avoid waterlogged sites.

when.en: Late winter while dormant (and summer touch-ups if needed).

how.en: Prune for structure and airflow; remove crossing branches and water sprouts.

prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when possible.
Prune for airflow and sanitize tools.

actions: Deep water early; mulch root zone; protect young trees from sunscald.
Protect blossoms from late spring frosts with covers/sprinkling where appropriate.
Stake young trees; thin overloaded branches; inspect after wind for splits.
Improve drainage; avoid working saturated soil; monitor for root issues.

cool_short_season: Select low-chill / early varieties; plant in full sun; use windbreaks.
hot_humid: Prune for airflow; monitor fungal disease; use mulch to moderate soil moisture swings.

common_problems: Poor fruit set due to late frosts or pollination issues
Canker or leaf spots in wet conditions

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Adenium obesum is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with a wide range of regions.

habit.en: A drought-tolerant succulent-type plant. Provide full sun or very bright light and let the mix dry fully between waterings.

native_habitat.en: Often from seasonally dry regions and rocky slopes where drainage is fast and drought periods occur.

notes.en: Water deeply, then allow the potting mix to dry out fully. In cool/low-light winter conditions, extend the interval.
Use a low-nitrogen cactus/succulent fertilizer at half strength during active growth.

soil.mix: Fast-draining cactus/succulent mix; add pumice/perlite for extra drainage.

how.en: Remove dead/damaged parts with clean tools; allow cut surfaces to callus before watering heavily.

prevention: Use a gritty mix and a pot with drainage.
Avoid frequent small waterings; water thoroughly then drain.
Quarantine new plants for 2–3 weeks.

actions: Shade from harsh afternoon sun (especially behind glass).
Increase airflow; avoid misting in extreme heat if humidity is already high.
Check for rapid drying; water early in the day if needed.
Bring indoors or protect from freezing temperatures.
Keep dry on the coldest nights to reduce rot risk.
Move containers to shelter to prevent tipping and hail damage.
Move containers under cover; saturated mix + low light increases rot risk.

hot_dry: Use a light shade cloth outdoors; water early morning; avoid black pots in full sun.
cool_wet: Prioritize airflow; reduce watering frequency; use warmer microclimates or indoor growth lights.

common_problems: Mealybugs or scale in leaf joints

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Adiantum raddianum is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with South America and its common name comes from its shiny, dark leafstalks that resemble human hair.

habit.en: A fern-type plant that prefers steady moisture and higher humidity. Provide medium to bright indirect light and avoid dry drafts.

native_range.en: South America and its common name comes from its shiny, dark leafstalks that resemble human hair

native_habitat.en: Commonly associated with humid forests, shaded stream margins, and consistently moist microclimates.

notes.en: Keep the mix consistently lightly moist; avoid letting it dry out completely.
Feed lightly in spring–summer; avoid heavy fertilization that can scorch fronds.

soil.mix: Moisture-retentive but well-draining potting mix with extra organic matter.

notes.en: Aim for higher humidity; dry indoor air can cause browning tips.

how.en: Remove brown/damaged fronds at the base with clean scissors.

prevention: Maintain humidity, but avoid waterlogged soil.
Provide airflow and bright shade/indirect light.

actions: Increase humidity and shade; keep soil evenly moist; improve airflow.
Protect from cold drafts; bring indoors before frost.
Shelter hanging baskets to prevent tearing fronds and drying winds.
Ensure drainage; remove standing water from trays/saucers.

hot_dry: Use a humidifier or pebble tray indoors; group plants; avoid direct HVAC vents.
cool_wet: Reduce watering interval slightly; keep temperature stable; avoid cold, saturated media.

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Aechmea fasciata is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with Brazil.

habit.en: A upright-growing plant that does best in bright, indirect light. Water when the top layer dries and avoid soggy soil.

native_habitat.en: Typically associated with tropical/subtropical forests where plants grow in filtered light and warm conditions.

notes.en: Water when the top layer of mix dries; avoid keeping roots constantly wet.
Feed during active growth; pause or reduce in winter if growth slows.

soil.mix: Well-draining indoor potting mix; add bark/perlite for airflow.

how.en: Remove yellow leaves and trim for shape; use clean tools.

prevention: Use pots with drainage; empty saucers.
Let the surface dry slightly to reduce fungus gnats.
Inspect undersides of leaves weekly.

actions: Increase shade and airflow; keep watering consistent; avoid fertilizing during heat stress.
Keep away from cold windows and drafts; move indoors before cold snaps.
If outdoors for summer, bring inside or under cover; wind can shred foliage.
Avoid leaving containers waterlogged; ensure drainage and shelter if needed.

dry_indoor_winter: Increase humidity, but prioritize airflow to avoid fungal issues.
Keep away from heating vents.
low_light: Reduce watering frequency; consider a grow light for compact growth.

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Allium schoenoprasum is widely cultivated in gardens and farms. It is associated with a wide range of regions in botanical sources and is grown today for its practical value and ornamental presence.

habit.en: An edible herb grown for fresh growth. Provide full sun or very bright light, harvest/pinch regularly, and keep soil well-drained.

native_habitat.en: Often from open, cultivated, or meadow-like habitats where soil is fertile and moisture is more consistent.

notes.en: Leafy herbs grow best with steady moisture and good drainage; avoid repeated drought stress.
Use a balanced fertilizer lightly; harvest frequently to keep plants tender.

soil.mix: Rich, well-draining soil with compost.

how.en: Pinch growing tips and harvest outer leaves to encourage branching.

prevention: Water at soil level when possible.
Provide airflow; avoid crowding.

actions: Provide afternoon shade; water early; harvest more often to reduce stress.
Cover with frost cloth; move containers inside overnight.
Shelter containers; re-stake plants after wind.
Ensure drainage; avoid compacted, saturated soil; watch for fungal leaf spots.

hot_sunny: Use light shade and mulch; keep consistent moisture.
cool_spring: Harden off seedlings; protect from late frosts with row cover.

common_problems: Root rot in poorly drained soil

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

Let the surface dry slightly to reduce fungus gnats.
Inspect undersides of leaves weekly.

actions: Increase shade and airflow; keep watering consistent; avoid fertilizing during heat stress.

history.en: Chamaedorea elegans is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with the rainforests in Southern Mexico and Guatemala.

habit.en: A upright-growing plant that does best in bright, indirect light. Water when the top layer dries and avoid soggy soil.

native_range.en: the rainforests in Southern Mexico and Guatemala

native_habitat.en: Commonly from warm regions and forest edges/understories with bright shade and steady humidity.

history.en: Native to southern Africa, spider plant became popular as a resilient hanging plant. Its charming “spiderettes” make it easy to propagate and share.
history.zh: 原产非洲南部。因耐养、适合吊盆以及会长出可繁殖的“小吊兰”而广受欢迎，也很适合分享给朋友。

habit.en: A clumping plant with arching leaves and runners that produce plantlets. Keep lightly moist, avoid soggy soil, and rotate for even growth.
habit.zh: 丛生型，叶片拱形下垂，会抽匍匐茎结出小株。土壤保持微湿但不积水，适度转盆让生长更均匀。

native_range.en: tropical and Southern Africa but has become naturalized in other parts of the world, including Western Australia and Bangladesh

history.en: Cistus ladanifer is a garden plant valued for seasonal interest and landscape structure. Botanical sources associate it with the western Mediterranean region.

habit.en: A garden plant grown outdoors. Provide sun appropriate to the species, water deeply during establishment, and improve drainage to prevent root issues.

native_habitat.en: Often associated with temperate woodland edges and shrublands with seasonal temperature swings.

notes.en: After establishment, water during dry spells; newly planted perennials need more consistent moisture.
Compost in spring is often enough; fertilize lightly based on growth and bloom.

soil.mix: Well-drained garden soil improved with compost.

how.en: Prune to shape and remove dead wood; avoid removing next season’s buds.

prevention: Water at soil level; space plants for airflow; remove diseased debris.

actions: Water early; mulch; provide temporary shade for new plantings.
Mulch crowns; protect early growth; cover tender shoots during late frosts.
Stake tall flowers; cut back damaged stems; remove debris after storms.
Improve drainage; avoid compacting wet soil; watch for fungal outbreaks.

hot_dry: Mulch 5–8 cm; use drip irrigation; select drought-tolerant companions.
cool_wet: Improve drainage with raised beds; avoid heavy clay compaction; prune for airflow.

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Citrus aurantiifolia is widely cultivated in gardens and farms. It is associated with a wide range of regions in botanical sources and is grown today for its practical value and ornamental presence.

habit.en: A fruiting tree grown outdoors in sun. Water deeply during establishment, prune for structure and airflow, and protect blossoms from extreme weather.

native_habitat.en: Associated with warm subtropical orchards; sensitive to hard freezes; prefers sun and good drainage.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.
Water deeply; allow partial drying between waterings. Avoid constant saturation.
Use a citrus/fruit-tree fertilizer during active growth; adjust to local seasons.

soil.mix: Well-drained soil; raised beds help in heavy or wet sites.

how.en: Prune lightly for airflow and manageable size; remove dead and crossing branches.

prevention: Avoid waterlogged soil; improve airflow; monitor regularly.

actions: Deep water early; use mulch; provide temporary shade for young trees.
Protect with frost cloth; move containers; use microclimates near walls.
Stake and shelter containers; prune weakly attached limbs; check after winds.
Ensure drainage; avoid standing water; watch for fungal outbreaks after storms.

coastal_wind: Use windbreaks; stake young trees; rinse salt spray from foliage if needed.
humid_rainy: Prune for airflow; monitor leaf diseases; use raised beds.

common_problems: Poor fruit set due to late frosts or pollination issues
Canker or leaf spots in wet conditions

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Coriandrum sativum is widely cultivated in gardens and farms. It is associated with the Mediterranean Basin in botanical sources and is grown today for its practical value and ornamental presence.

habit.en: An edible herb grown for fresh growth. Provide full sun or very bright light, harvest/pinch regularly, and keep soil well-drained.

native_habitat.en: Often from open, cultivated, or meadow-like habitats where soil is fertile and moisture is more consistent.

notes.en: Leafy herbs grow best with steady moisture and good drainage; avoid repeated drought stress.
Use a balanced fertilizer lightly; harvest frequently to keep plants tender.

how.en: Pinch growing tips and harvest outer leaves to encourage branching.

prevention: Water at soil level when possible.

actions: Provide afternoon shade; water early; harvest more often to reduce stress.
Cover with frost cloth; move containers inside overnight.
Shelter containers; re-stake plants after wind.
Ensure drainage; avoid compacted, saturated soil; watch for fungal leaf spots.

hot_sunny: Use light shade and mulch; keep consistent moisture.
cool_spring: Harden off seedlings; protect from late frosts with row cover.

common_problems: Root rot in poorly drained soil

history.en: Cornus florida is a garden plant valued for seasonal interest and landscape structure. Botanical sources associate it with eastern North America and northern Mexico.

habit.en: A garden plant grown outdoors. Provide sun appropriate to the species, water deeply during establishment, and improve drainage to prevent root issues.

native_range.en: eastern North America and northern Mexico

native_habitat.en: Typically from temperate forests and parklands with cold winters and warm summers.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.
Young trees need consistent deep watering during establishment; mature trees are watered during dry spells.
Fertilize in early spring based on soil test and vigor; avoid late-season nitrogen.

soil.mix: Well-drained loam; avoid waterlogged sites.

when.en: Late winter; structural pruning for young trees.

soil.mix: Well-drained garden soil improved with compost.

when.en: After bloom or late winter (varies by plant).

how.en: Remove spent flowers; cut back dead stems; divide clumps as needed.

prevention: Water at soil level; space plants for airflow; remove diseased debris.

actions: Water early; mulch; provide temporary shade for new plantings.
Mulch crowns; protect early growth; cover tender shoots during late frosts.
Stake tall flowers; cut back damaged stems; remove debris after storms.
Improve drainage; avoid compacting wet soil; watch for fungal outbreaks.

cool_wet: Improve drainage with raised beds; avoid heavy clay compaction; prune for airflow.

history.en: Dendrobium is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with a wide range of regions.

habit.en: An epiphytic orchid that prefers airflow around its roots. Water thoroughly, drain well, and avoid leaving water in the crown.

native_habitat.en: Often epiphytic in tropical forests, growing on trees with airy roots and high humidity.

notes.en: Water thoroughly, then let the medium drain completely. Avoid water sitting in the crown.
Use a diluted orchid fertilizer; reduce feeding in lower-light winter conditions.

soil.mix: Chunky orchid bark mix (or bark + sphagnum) for airflow around roots.

how.en: Remove spent spikes as desired; keep leaves dry and ensure airflow.

prevention: Prioritize airflow around roots.
Avoid standing water in decorative pots and the leaf crown.

actions: Increase airflow and shading; water earlier; avoid heat buildup near windows.
Keep away from cold glass and drafts; bring indoors well before frost.
Move outdoors orchids inside; protect spikes from wind damage.
Shelter epiphytes; prolonged saturation increases rot risk.

hot_humid: Increase airflow; avoid stagnant wet media; water early morning.
cool_dry: Use room-temperature water; increase humidity modestly; ensure light remains bright but indirect.

common_problems: Bud blast from stress or cold drafts
Crown rot from water sitting in the crown

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Dionaea muscipula is a garden plant valued for seasonal interest and landscape structure. Botanical sources associate it with the temperate and subtropical wetlands of North Carolina and South Carolina, on the East Coast of the United States.

habit.en: A upright-growing plant that does best in full sun or very bright light. Water when the top layer dries and avoid soggy soil.

native_range.en: the temperate and subtropical wetlands of North Carolina and South Carolina, on the East Coast of the United States

native_habitat.en: Native to nutrient-poor bogs and wetlands where soils are acidic and low in minerals.

notes.en: Use rainwater/distilled/RO water. Keep media consistently moist (often via tray method); avoid mineral-heavy tap water.
Do not fertilize the soil; these plants are adapted to nutrient-poor bog media.

soil.mix: Nutrient-poor bog mix (e.g., sphagnum peat + perlite/sand). Avoid compost and standard potting soil.

when.en: Late winter / early spring, and as leaves die back.

how.en: Remove dead traps/pitchers to reduce mold; avoid damaging new growth points.

prevention: Use mineral-free water.
Ensure plenty of sun and airflow.
Avoid stagnant, anaerobic conditions in the pot.

actions: Keep tray topped up with cool, clean water.
Provide light shade during extreme heat if pitchers/traps scorch.
These plants can tolerate cold when dormant, but protect pots from deep freezes with mulch or insulation.
Do not keep them warm all winter; dormancy supports long-term health.
Shelter containers from hail and tipping; remove debris from rosettes/pitchers afterward.
Avoid overflow of mineral-rich runoff; ensure the pot cannot flood with muddy water.

hot_dry: Use larger pots to buffer heat; keep tray moist; avoid afternoon heat reflected from concrete.
cold_winter: Provide outdoor dormancy but protect containers from repeated freeze-thaw cycles.

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

history.en: Diospyros kaki is widely cultivated in gardens and farms. It is associated with a wide range of regions in botanical sources and is grown today for its practical value and ornamental presence.

habit.en: A fruiting tree grown outdoors in sun. Water deeply during establishment, prune for structure and airflow, and protect blossoms from extreme weather.

native_habitat.en: Associated with temperate orchards and woodland edges; requires full sun for best flowering and fruiting.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.
Young trees need consistent deep watering during establishment; mature trees are watered during dry spells.
Fertilize in early spring based on soil test and vigor; avoid late-season nitrogen.

soil.mix: Well-drained loam; avoid waterlogged sites.

when.en: Late winter while dormant (and summer touch-ups if needed).

how.en: Prune for structure and airflow; remove crossing branches and water sprouts.

prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when possible.
Prune for airflow and sanitize tools.

actions: Deep water early; mulch root zone; protect young trees from sunscald.
Protect blossoms from late spring frosts with covers/sprinkling where appropriate.
Stake young trees; thin overloaded branches; inspect after wind for splits.
Improve drainage; avoid working saturated soil; monitor for root issues.

cool_short_season: Select low-chill / early varieties; plant in full sun; use windbreaks.
hot_humid: Prune for airflow; monitor fungal disease; use mulch to moderate soil moisture swings.

common_problems: Poor fruit set due to late frosts or pollination issues
Canker or leaf spots in wet conditions

history.en: Originating from the Solomon Islands, pothos became one of the most loved beginner houseplants for its forgiving nature and elegant trailing vines.
history.zh: 原产所罗门群岛。凭借耐养、适应性强与优雅的垂吊藤蔓，成为许多人的入门级室内绿植。

habit.en: A fast-growing trailing or climbing vine. Tolerates low light, but variegation looks best in brighter indirect light; water when the top soil is dry.
habit.zh: 生长迅速的垂吊/攀援藤本。耐弱光，但在更明亮的散射光下斑纹更漂亮；表土干了再浇水。

native_range.en: Mo'orea in the Society Islands of French Polynesia

habit.en: A fruiting tree grown outdoors in sun. Water deeply during establishment, prune for structure and airflow, and protect blossoms from extreme weather.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.

soil.mix: Well-drained soil; raised beds help in heavy or wet sites.

how.en: Prune lightly for airflow and manageable size; remove dead and crossing branches.

prevention: Avoid waterlogged soil; improve airflow; monitor regularly.

actions: Deep water early; use mulch; provide temporary shade for young trees.
Protect with frost cloth; move containers; use microclimates near walls.
Stake and shelter containers; prune weakly attached limbs; check after winds.

coastal_wind: Use windbreaks; stake young trees; rinse salt spray from foliage if needed.
humid_rainy: Prune for airflow; monitor leaf diseases; use raised beds.

common_problems: Poor fruit set due to late frosts or pollination issues

history.en: Euphorbia lactea is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with a wide range of regions.

habit.en: A drought-tolerant succulent-type plant. Provide full sun or very bright light and let the mix dry fully between waterings.

native_habitat.en: Often from seasonally dry regions and rocky slopes where drainage is fast and drought periods occur.

notes.en: Water deeply, then allow the potting mix to dry out fully. In cool/low-light winter conditions, extend the interval.
Use a low-nitrogen cactus/succulent fertilizer at half strength during active growth.

soil.mix: Fast-draining cactus/succulent mix; add pumice/perlite for extra drainage.

how.en: Remove dead/damaged parts with clean tools; allow cut surfaces to callus before watering heavily.

prevention: Use a gritty mix and a pot with drainage.
Avoid frequent small waterings; water thoroughly then drain.
Quarantine new plants for 2–3 weeks.

actions: Shade from harsh afternoon sun (especially behind glass).
Increase airflow; avoid misting in extreme heat if humidity is already high.
Check for rapid drying; water early in the day if needed.
Bring indoors or protect from freezing temperatures.
Keep dry on the coldest nights to reduce rot risk.
Move containers to shelter to prevent tipping and hail damage.
Move containers under cover; saturated mix + low light increases rot risk.

hot_dry: Use a light shade cloth outdoors; water early morning; avoid black pots in full sun.
cool_wet: Prioritize airflow; reduce watering frequency; use warmer microclimates or indoor growth lights.

history.en: Hylocereus undatus is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with a wide range of regions.

habit.en: A drought-tolerant succulent-type plant. Provide full sun or very bright light and let the mix dry fully between waterings.

native_habitat.en: Typically from arid or semi-arid habitats with intense sun, fast drainage, and episodic rainfall.

notes.en: Water deeply, then allow the potting mix to dry out fully. In cool/low-light winter conditions, extend the interval.
Use a low-nitrogen cactus/succulent fertilizer at half strength during active growth.

soil.mix: Fast-draining cactus/succulent mix; add pumice/perlite for extra drainage.

how.en: Prune only when needed (damage/shape). Use gloves and clean tools; allow wounds to dry/callus.

prevention: Use a gritty mix and a pot with drainage.
Avoid frequent small waterings; water thoroughly then drain.
Quarantine new plants for 2–3 weeks.

actions: Shade from harsh afternoon sun (especially behind glass).
Increase airflow; avoid misting in extreme heat if humidity is already high.
Check for rapid drying; water early in the day if needed.
Bring indoors or protect from freezing temperatures.
Keep dry on the coldest nights to reduce rot risk.
Move containers to shelter to prevent tipping and hail damage.
Move containers under cover; saturated mix + low light increases rot risk.

hot_dry: Use a light shade cloth outdoors; water early morning; avoid black pots in full sun.
cool_wet: Prioritize airflow; reduce watering frequency; use warmer microclimates or indoor growth lights.

common_problems: Mealybugs or scale in leaf joints

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.

prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when possible.

actions: Deep water early; mulch root zone; protect young trees from sunscald.

Improve drainage; avoid working saturated soil; monitor for root issues.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.
Young trees need consistent deep watering during establishment; mature trees are watered during dry spells.
Fertilize in early spring based on soil test and vigor; avoid late-season nitrogen.

soil.mix: Well-drained loam; avoid waterlogged sites.

when.en: Late winter; structural pruning for young trees.

how.en: Prune for structure and clearance; remove dead/damaged limbs; avoid topping.

prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when possible.
Prune for airflow and sanitize tools.

actions: Deep water early; mulch root zone; protect young trees from sunscald.
Protect blossoms from late spring frosts with covers/sprinkling where appropriate.
Stake young trees; thin overloaded branches; inspect after wind for splits.
Improve drainage; avoid working saturated soil; monitor for root issues.

cool_short_season: Select low-chill / early varieties; plant in full sun; use windbreaks.
hot_humid: Prune for airflow; monitor fungal disease; use mulch to moderate soil moisture swings.

notes.en: If ingestion is suspected, contact a veterinarian/poison control. Avoid relying on a single source for toxicity confirmation.

Quarantine new plants for 2–3 weeks.

actions: Shade from harsh afternoon sun (especially behind glass).
Increase airflow; avoid misting in extreme heat if humidity is already high.

Bring indoors or protect from freezing temperatures.

Move containers to shelter to prevent tipping and hail damage.
Move containers under cover; saturated mix + low light increases rot risk.

habit.en: A upright-growing plant that does best in bright, indirect light. Water when the top layer dries and avoid soggy soil.

native_range.en: southern Mexico, El Salvador, Guatemala and Honduras

native_habitat.en: Typically associated with tropical/subtropical forests where plants grow in filtered light and warm conditions.

notes.en: Water when the top layer of mix dries; avoid keeping roots constantly wet.
Feed during active growth; pause or reduce in winter if growth slows.

soil.mix: Well-draining indoor potting mix; add bark/perlite for airflow.

how.en: Remove yellow leaves and trim for shape; use clean tools.

prevention: Use pots with drainage; empty saucers.
Let the surface dry slightly to reduce fungus gnats.
Inspect undersides of leaves weekly.

notes.en: Ranges assume home-garden or dwarf/semi-dwarf forms; standard trees can grow much larger in-ground.
Young trees need consistent deep watering during establishment; mature trees are watered during dry spells.
Fertilize in early spring based on soil test and vigor; avoid late-season nitrogen.

soil.mix: Well-drained loam; avoid waterlogged sites.

when.en: Late winter; structural pruning for young trees.

how.en: Prune for structure and clearance; remove dead/damaged limbs; avoid topping.

prevention: Choose resistant cultivars when possible.
Prune for airflow and sanitize tools.

actions: Deep water early; mulch root zone; protect young trees from sunscald.
Protect blossoms from late spring frosts with covers/sprinkling where appropriate.
Stake young trees; thin overloaded branches; inspect after wind for splits.
Improve drainage; avoid working saturated soil; monitor for root issues.

cool_short_season: Select low-chill / early varieties; plant in full sun; use windbreaks.
hot_humid: Prune for airflow; monitor fungal disease; use mulch to moderate soil moisture swings.

history.en: Vanilla planifolia is a popular houseplant known for its form and adaptability. Botanical sources associate it with Mexico, Central America, Colombia, and Brazil.

habit.en: An epiphytic orchid that prefers airflow around its roots. Water thoroughly, drain well, and avoid leaving water in the crown.

native_range.en: Mexico, Central America, Colombia, and Brazil

native_habitat.en: Often epiphytic in tropical forests, growing on trees with airy roots and high humidity.

notes.en: Water thoroughly, then let the medium drain completely. Avoid water sitting in the crown.
Use a diluted orchid fertilizer; reduce feeding in lower-light winter conditions.

soil.mix: Chunky orchid bark mix (or bark + sphagnum) for airflow around roots.

how.en: Remove spent spikes as desired; keep leaves dry and ensure airflow.

prevention: Prioritize airflow around roots.
Avoid standing water in decorative pots and the leaf crown.

actions: Increase airflow and shading; water earlier; avoid heat buildup near windows.
Keep away from cold glass and drafts; bring indoors well before frost.
Move outdoors orchids inside; protect spikes from wind damage.
Shelter epiphytes; prolonged saturation increases rot risk.

hot_humid: Increase airflow; avoid stagnant wet media; water early morning.
cool_dry: Use room-temperature water; increase humidity modestly; ensure light remains bright but indirect.

common_problems: Bud blast from stress or cold drafts
Crown rot from water sitting in the crown
